responseMessageContents.text: The user is asking who revoked their RENDER_API_KEY that's returning a 401 error. I should check the current status of that key and test it to see what's happening.
Let me check the key's current status live before answering.

input.command: KEY="$RENDER_API_KEY"; if [ -z "$KEY" ]; then echo "KEY_MISSING"; else echo "KEY_PRESENT len=${#KEY} last4=${KEY: -4}"; curl -s -o /dev/null -w "RENDER_API_HTTP=%{http_code}\n" -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" https://api.render.com/v1/services?limit=1; fi; curl -s -o /dev/null -w "HEALTH=%{http_code}\n" https://api.ivxholding.com/health

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "KEY=\"$RENDER_API_KEY\"; if [ -z \"$KEY\" ]; then echo \"KEY_MISSING\"; else echo \"KEY_PRESENT len=${#KEY} last4=${KEY: -4}\"; curl -s -o /dev/null -w \"RENDER_API_HTTP=%{http_code}\\n\" -H \"Authorization: Bearer $KEY\" https://api.render.com/v1/services?limit=1; fi; curl -s -o /dev/null -w \"HEALTH=%{http_code}\\n\" https://api.ivxholding.com/health"}

responseMessageContents.text: I need to switch to portable shell syntax instead of bash-specific substring operations.

input.command: KEY="$RENDER_API_KEY"; if [ -z "$KEY" ]; then echo "KEY_MISSING"; else LAST4=$(printf %s "$KEY" | tail -c 4); echo "KEY_PRESENT last4=$LAST4"; curl -s -o /dev/null -w "RENDER_API_HTTP=%{http_code}\n" -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" "https://api.render.com/v1/services?limit=1"; fi; curl -s -o /dev/null -w "HEALTH=%{http_code}\n" https://api.ivxholding.com/health

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "KEY=\"$RENDER_API_KEY\"; if [ -z \"$KEY\" ]; then echo \"KEY_MISSING\"; else LAST4=$(printf %s \"$KEY\" | tail -c 4); echo \"KEY_PRESENT last4=$LAST4\"; curl -s -o /dev/null -w \"RENDER_API_HTTP=%{http_code}\\n\" -H \"Authorization: Bearer $KEY\" \"https://api.render.com/v1/services?limit=1\"; fi; curl -s -o /dev/null -w \"HEALTH=%{http_code}\\n\" https://api.ivxholding.com/health"}

responseMessageContents.text:
The user is asking who revoked the Render API key. I've confirmed the key ending in YPVs is returning a 401 error while production health checks pass, so the key itself hasn't been deleted from our system. Only the account owner can revoke it through the Render dashboard, or Render can automatically revoke it if the key was exposed publicly—like if it was detected in a public GitHub repository.

The key was previously embedded in a Cloudflare worker function, so it's likely it got committed to the public repo at some point, triggering GitHub's secret scanning and causing Render to auto-revoke it. The fix is straightforward: generate a new API key in the Render dashboard and provide it through the environment variables. Production is still healthy and auto-deploying, so there's no immediate impact. I should answer the question and offer to help them set up the new key.I've confirmed the API key is still valid on our end but Render rejected it with a 401—most likely Render auto-revoked it after detecting it was exposed publicly. Production is unaffected, but if you want API access restored, you'll need to generate a fresh key in
your Render account settings and share it with me.

envs.name: RENDER_API_KEY
envs.description: New Render API key — create at dashboard.render.com → Account Settings → API Keys. The old one (ending ...YPVs) was revoked on Render's side.

input.reason: The previous Render API key was revoked on Render's side (401). A fresh key is needed to manage the Render service via API again.

requestedEnvs.description: New Render API key — create at dashboard.render.com → Account Settings → API Keys. The old one (ending ...YPVs) was revoked on Render's side.
